ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers whole grape; green variety is a cultivar subset. Match is defensible as the closest available FoodOn term.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of green grapes is approximately 120 g (about 15 medium grapes), commonly consumed as a snack or dessert accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Green Grapes — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for green grapes (F04010049). All core macros and micronutrients reflect the HPB record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 46 for green/white grapes is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 46 × 18.72 / 100 ≈ 8.6, using 120 g serving with 15.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High natural sugar (15.2 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite zero sodium and beneficial potassium (220 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Limit green grape portions to 80–100 g per occasion and pair with a protein or fibre source to blunt the glycaemic and blood-pressure impact of the high sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 15.2 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 0.8 g/100 g exceeds the diabetes sugar threshold (>15 g/100 g with fibre <2 g/100 g), producing a rapid postprandial glucose rise despite a low GI of 46
type2_diabetes.guidance: Restrict portions to approximately 80 g per serving, pair with protein or unsaturated fat to slow glucose absorption, and prefer lower-sugar fruits such as berries, guava, or apple for daily consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero sodium, and meaningful potassium (220 mg/100 g) support cardiovascular health; polyphenols including resveratrol precursors and quercetin provide antioxidant benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Green grapes are cardioprotective in moderate portions (80–120 g) due to their favourable lipid and sodium profile and polyphenol content, but high sugar intake should be managed to avoid triglyceride elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High natural sugar (15.2 g/100 g) can promote systemic inflammation at large portions; polyphenols (resveratrol precursors, quercetin) offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit but fibre is insufficient to qualify as a positive RA factor
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Consume in moderation (80–100 g per serving); the anti-inflammatory polyphenol content is beneficial but does not offset the pro-inflammatory risk of excess sugar consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green grapes (Grape, green; F04010049) are fresh, whole table grapes with a light green-to-yellow skin, firm and juicy flesh, and a sweet-crisp flavour profile. They are consumed raw as a snack, dessert component, or fruit platter element and require no preparation beyond washing. Per 100 g, green grapes provide 65 kcal, 15.6 g carbohydrate (of which 15.2 g is natural sugar), 0.8 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 0.8 g dietary fibre, 220 mg potassium, 10 mg calcium, 0.36 mg iron, and zero sodium. The glycemic index is 46 (low), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 8.6 per 120 g serving. Despite a low GI, the high sugar-to-fibre ratio (15.2 g sugar with only 0.8 g fibre per 100 g) means rapid glucose absorption is expected, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. Green grapes contain polyphenols including resveratrol precursors and quercetin, which confer antioxidant and modest anti-inflammatory properties relevant to cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. The zero sodium and zero saturated fat profile is favourable for cardiovascular health. Commonly available worldwide and widely consumed in Singapore as a fresh snack. Suitable for plant-based, vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: Are green grapes good for diabetics?
How many calories in green grapes?
Green grapes for high blood pressure
Green grapes vs red grapes nutrition
How many grapes in a serving?

confidence_reason: All core macros and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (F04010049) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 46 is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Source match is exact for both nutrient and GI data.
notes: Fat_g is 0.0: green grapes are naturally fat-free; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F04010049 and consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for raw grapes. Saturated_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a raw fruit with no fat content. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant food. Sodium_mg is 0.0: naturally zero for fresh unsalted fruit, confirmed per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo F04010049 record; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 09132, raw grapes) may be consulted for supplementary micronutrient data in future enrichment. GI of 46 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). GL per 120 g serving = 46 × (15.6 × 1.2) / 100 = 46 × 18.72 / 100 ≈ 8.6. This is a generic fresh fruit record; no cuisine-specific multilingual naming is required as green grapes are a globally consumed raw fruit not specific to any single cuisine.
